source: All-In Podcast — DRAM bottleneck, AI infrastructure, Chinese open source models
summary: Terrestrial data center capacity that does get built will become extremely valuable ('hope diamonds') as contestation rates rise
summary_extended: Baker argues that terrestrial data center capacity will become extraordinarily scarce and valuable — likening successfully permitted and energized facilities to 'hope diamonds' — because roughly 40% of planned data centers since 2021 have been contested or blocked, and he expects this contestation rate to increase going forward. The core reasoning is that community opposition, permitting hurdles, and power availability constraints are creating a structural bottleneck: the more data center demand grows, the harder it becomes to add new supply, making existing permitted and energized capacity disproportionately valuable. Baker specifically highlights facilities in the hundreds-of-megawatts to gigawatt scale as the most coveted, as hyperscalers and AI companies require massive contiguous power blocks that are nearly impossible to replicate in contested markets. The investment implication is that publicly traded operators of large-scale, already-permitted and energized data center campuses — particularly colocation REITs with established power agreements — stand to benefit from rising contestation rates as pricing power and occupancy improve structurally.
detail: Baker estimates ~40% of planned data centers since 2021 have been contested/blocked, and expects this rate to increase. This makes successfully permitted and energized terrestrial compute capacity scarce and highly valuable, particularly in the hundreds of megawatts to gigawatt range.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses the 40% contestation figure and 'hope diamonds' framing with conviction, but the forward projection is inherently uncertain
quote_fragment: whatever is left on the ground will be incredibly valuable. It'll be a diamond

ticker_audit.reasoning: Equinix operates large-scale colocation data centers that are already permitted and energized; directly benefits from scarcity of new terrestrial compute capacity as existing operators gain pricing power.

Digital Realty is a major data center REIT with large existing campuses; directly benefits from the structural scarcity thesis as new supply faces increasing contestation and its existing permitted capacity becomes more valuable.

Iron Mountain's data center segment (large-scale, already-permitted facilities) is a direct beneficiary of scarcity in terrestrial compute capacity, though its core business is records management — added because its data center footprint is substantial and unambiguous.
QTS was taken private by Blackstone in 2021 and is no longer US-listed; cannot be included as a US-listed equity beneficiary.
